facts.statement: Hash table load factor threshold for chaining is typically 1.0; for linear probing, it should be < 0.67 to maintain performance
When a hash table resizes using geometric growth (doubling), any sequence of n operations takes O(n) total time, not O(n²), despite occasional O(n) rehashing operations
Java ArrayList uses a growth factor of 1.5 (not 2.0), implemented via bitwise left shift (capacity = (capacity * 3) / 2) to balance memory waste and reallocation frequency
AVL trees maintain height O(1.44 * log n) while red-black trees permit height O(2 * log n), making AVL 1.44x shorter on average for the same node count
Red-black trees require at most 2 rotations per insertion and 3 per deletion, while AVL trees may require O(log n) rotations, making red-black preferred for insertion-heavy workloads
Binary heaps stored in arrays are 20-35% faster than BSTs in practice due to contiguous memory layout and cache-friendly traversal
Constructing a heap from 1,000,000 elements takes O(n) time (~25-40ms on modern JS runtime); inserting into a BST one-by-one is 3-6x slower due to pointer traversal
Trie prefix lookups are slower than hash table lookups (20-40% overhead) when random-access memory dominates, but superior for ordered traversal and autocomplete
Memoization top-down DP reduces time from O(2^n) to O(n) but maintains O(n) space overhead from cache + recursion stack
Bottom-up tabulation DP eliminates recursion overhead; further space optimization to O(1) is possible when current state depends on fixed-size previous states (e.g., Fibonacci)
Binary search on answer applies when the solution space is monotonic: 'Is valid solution ≤ X?' remains true or false as X increases, enabling log n search over answer range
Parametric search extends binary search to optimize over problem parameters (e.g., finding threshold that minimizes cost function), but is harder to implement than direct binary search
Linear probing has best cache locality but suffers primary clustering; double hashing avoids clustering but has poor cache performance; quadratic probing balances both
Bloom filters require 9.6 bits per element for 1% false positive rate; Redis hash table requires ~320 bits per element (IP address); 33x more space for hash table equivalent
Two O(n) algorithms can differ by 5-10x in execution time due to constant factors, cache effects, and input distribution that big-O notation obscures
Insertion sort beats merge sort on small arrays (<50 elements) despite O(n²) worst case, due to hidden constant factors; standard libaries exploit this with hybrid approaches
Cache-oblivious B-trees outperform traditional disk-access B-trees by >2x on random workloads by recursively dividing problems to fit into cache hierarchy without knowing cache size
Fibonacci heap extract-min is O(log n) amortized despite O(trees(H)) actual worst-case cost; potential function mates lazy consolidation with deferred balancing
Dynamic array doubling amortizes cost: total copies for n inserts = n * 2 / (2-1) = 2n; amortized cost per insert = 3 (1 insert + avg 2 copy operations from prior resizes)
Geometric growth (doubling) for hash table resizing is critical: linear growth (+100 buckets per resize) leads to O(n²) total cost instead of O(n)

examples.title: Java Collections Library hash table resizing strategy
examples.description: HashMap uses load factor 0.75; when exceeded, capacity doubles from e.g. 16 → 32 buckets. All 16+ elements are rehashed in O(n) time. Next 32 cheap inserts follow before next O(n) rehash at factor 0.75 again.
examples.metric: Total time for 1000 inserts: O(1000), not O(1000²)

examples.title: Linux kernel red-black tree for process scheduling
examples.description: Kernel uses red-black trees in CFS scheduler to balance process priority queues. Red-black chosen over AVL due to fewer rotations on insert/delete, reducing lock contention in concurrent workloads.
examples.metric: O(log n) insertion with ≤3 rotations; AVL may rotate O(log n) times

examples.title: Priority queue implementation choice: Fibonacci heap vs binary heap
examples.description: Fibonacci heaps excel in Dijkstra's algorithm: O(E + V log V) with Fib heap vs O((E + V) log V) with binary heap on dense graphs. Fib heap's O(1) amortized decrease-key avoids percolate-down on every priority update.
examples.metric: Real-world graph routing: 30-40% faster on 10^5 node graphs with dense edges

examples.title: Cloudflare Bloom filter for DDoS mitigation
examples.description: Cloudflare uses Bloom filters to check IP reputation at edge. Single bit-check per packet instead of hash table lookup saves memory on 200M+ suspect IPs: ~1.2GB vs ~80GB for hash table.
examples.metric: 0.01% false positive rate; 330x memory savings vs equivalent hash table

examples.title: Google's cache-oblivious sorting in MapReduce
examples.description: MapReduce shuffle phase uses cache-oblivious merge strategies to sort across multi-level memory (L1/L2/L3/RAM/disk). Recursively divides partitions until they fit L3 cache, avoiding explicit tuning per machine type.
examples.metric: Same algorithm runs 2-3x faster on different CPU architectures without code changes

examples.title: Dynamic programming in edit distance (Levenshtein)
examples.description: Naive recursive solution: O(3^(m+n)) time. Memoized top-down: O(m*n) time, O(m*n) space. Bottom-up tabulation: O(m*n) time, O(m*n) space. Space-optimized bottom-up: O(m*n) time, O(min(m,n)) space.
examples.metric: Strings of length 1000: naive timeout; memoized ~1ms; space-optimized 1ms with 1KB vs 1MB memory

analogies.analogy: Amortized cost is like averaging your commute time over a month: one day the road is closed and you take 2 hours (worst-case), but 29 days you take 30 minutes, averaging 33 minutes. Big-O worst-case reports only the 2-hour day; amortized analysis reports the 33-minute average.
analogies.applies_to: Hash table resizing, dynamic arrays, Fibonacci heaps

analogies.concept: Load factor and hash table performance
analogies.analogy: A hash table is like a parking lot. Load factor 0.5 = half full, cars park quickly. Load factor 0.9 = 90% full, drivers circle looking for spaces (probing/chaining grows). Too high and your O(1) 'find parking' becomes O(log n) or worse.
analogies.applies_to: Understanding why 0.75 is a sweet spot for Java HashMap

analogies.analogy: AVL is a perfectionist: rebalances aggressively after every change, keeping the tree nearly perfect but spending lots of time on rebalancing. Red-black is pragmatic: tolerates minor imbalance to avoid excessive rebalancing. For read-heavy code, hire the perfectionist; for edit-heavy code, hire the pragmatist.
analogies.applies_to: Choosing tree structures for different workload patterns

analogies.analogy: Instead of searching a sorted array [1,2,3,5,8], imagine searching for the largest X where f(X) is true. You don't have the array; you just know f(1)=true, f(2)=true, f(3)=true, f(4)=false. Binary search still finds the boundary at 3 in O(log range) time.
analogies.applies_to: Optimization problems, parametric search, feasibility testing

analogies.analogy: Memoization (top-down) is like asking your friend for help, who asks another friend, who asks another (recursion) — efficient but uses stack space. Bottom-up DP is like building from the ground floor up — no recursion, just iteration, same time but your stack stays clean.
analogies.applies_to: Space optimization strategies in dynamic programming

analogies.analogy: A cache-oblivious algorithm is like a chess strategy that works against all opponents without knowing their skill level. Cache-aware algorithms are tuned for specific CPU caches, like training specifically for one opponent. Cache-oblivious scales transparently across L1/L2/L3/RAM without modification.
analogies.applies_to: Performance on heterogeneous hardware and multi-level memory

analogies.analogy: A Bloom filter is like a bouncer at an exclusive club. It says 'You're definitely NOT on the list' (100% accurate) or 'You MIGHT be on the list' (1% might be imposters). A hash table bouncer says 'You're either on the list or not' (100% accurate) but keeps a massive guest book.
analogies.applies_to: Space vs accuracy tradeoffs in membership testing

Multiply capacity by 1.5 on resize; balances memory waste (vs 2.0) with reallocation frequency

key_numbers.context: Each insert costs ~3 on average (1 insert + 2 units of amortized copy cost from resizes)
AVL max height = 1.44 * log(n); red-black max height = 2.0 * log(n)

key_numbers.context: Bounded recoloring + rotations; AVL may need O(log n) rotations
Building heap from 1M elements: 25-40ms; BST one-by-one insert: 75-240ms

key_numbers.metric: Cache speedup of linear probing over double hashing
key_numbers.context: Linear probing's cache-friendly sequential access vs double hashing's random probing

key_numbers.metric: Bloom filter bits per element (1% false positive)
key_numbers.context: vs ~320 bits for hash table equivalent (33x less memory)

key_numbers.metric: Bloom filter bits per element (0.01% false positive)
key_numbers.context: More stringent false positive requirement; still 16x smaller than hash table
Two O(n) algorithms can differ this much due to constant factors and cache effects

key_numbers.metric: Insertion sort threshold in hybrid sorts
key_numbers.context: Elements; insertion sort beats merge sort below this threshold despite O(n²) worst case

key_numbers.metric: Cache-oblivious B-tree speedup vs traditional B-tree
key_numbers.context: Random insert/query workloads; scales without tuning across L1/L2/L3/RAM
vs O(log n) for binary heap; critical for Dijkstra on dense graphs
Typical optimized JS runtime; affects practical choice between hash vs tree vs trie

common_misconceptions.misconception: O(1) hash table operations mean every access is equally fast
common_misconceptions.reality: O(1) is amortized; individual operations vary from 1ns (empty bucket) to microseconds (collision chain traversal). Resize operations are O(n). Performance depends on load factor and hash function quality.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Higher load factor (e.g., 0.99) is fine because operations are still O(1) amortized
common_misconceptions.reality: While amortized complexity holds, constant factors explode. Collision chains grow O(load_factor / (1 - load_factor)); at 0.99, expect 99 probes on average vs 4 at 0.75. Amortized doesn't mean practical.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Always double the array size on resize to maximize amortized efficiency
common_misconceptions.reality: Doubling wastes up to 50% memory. Java's 1.5x multiplier and C++'s vary between 1.5-2.0. The key is geometric growth (constant ratio); linear growth (+100 slots) leads to O(n²) total cost.

common_misconceptions.misconception: AVL trees are always better because they're more balanced
common_misconceptions.reality: Strict balancing (AVL) adds rebalancing overhead. Red-black trees are faster for insertion/deletion-heavy workloads due to fewer rotations. The choice depends on access pattern (read vs write dominant).

common_misconceptions.misconception: Binary heaps are slower than BSTs because they lack ordering
common_misconceptions.reality: Heaps are 20-35% faster in practice due to contiguous array storage (cache-friendly). Lack of full ordering isn't a weakness—it's the reason the heap is compact and fast.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Memoization is always better than bottom-up DP because top-down is more intuitive
common_misconceptions.reality: Memoization keeps the recursion stack alive, wasting O(n) memory on stack frames. Bottom-up iteration avoids this. Neither is universally better—choose based on space budget.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Linear probing is the worst hash collision resolution because of clustering
common_misconceptions.reality: Linear probing is cache-optimal and practical for moderate load factors. The problem is poor load factor management or weak hash functions, not the probing strategy itself.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Bloom filters always use less memory than hash tables for the same data
common_misconceptions.reality: Bloom filters save memory (9.6 bits/element vs 320 bits) but can't retrieve values, only test membership. If you need values, you still need a hash table or external store.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Big-O notation is sufficient for performance comparison
common_misconceptions.reality: Big-O hides constant factors, which can be 5-10x. Two O(n) algorithms may differ drastically. Use profiling (perf, VTune, cProfile) to measure actual execution time.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Binary search only works on sorted arrays
common_misconceptions.reality: Binary search applies to any monotonic search space. You can binary search over answer ranges (feasibility), parameter spaces, or custom predicates—not just array indices.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Tries are faster than hash tables for all prefix-based lookups
common_misconceptions.reality: Hash tables beat tries on random access (20-40% faster) but lose on prefix iteration and autocomplete. Tries excel for lexicographic traversal and range queries.
Cache-oblivious algorithms avoid tuning and scale transparently but may underperform hand-tuned cache-aware algorithms on specific hardware. They're 'good enough' for heterogeneous systems.
